title: Christianity
summary: The religious movement that began as a Jewish sect in Roman Palestine, became the official religion of the Roman Empire, shaped Western civilization's law, art, philosophy, and political thought for two thousand years, and now claims more than two billion adherents worldwide — the largest and most geographically diverse religion in history.
hook: Christianity began as a rumor in a province of the Roman Empire: that a executed Jew had risen from the dead. Within three centuries, that rumor had become the official religion of the empire that killed him. Within fifteen centuries, it had reshaped every aspect of European civilization. Understanding why is one of the central questions of world history.

beginner.thePeriod:
Christianity is the religion centered on Jesus of Nazareth — a Jewish preacher in Roman Palestine who was crucified around 30 CE. His followers believed he rose from the dead, and this belief became the foundation of a new religious movement. Within a generation, small communities of Christians had formed in cities across the Roman Empire. Within three centuries it had become Rome's official religion.

For most of the two thousand years since, Christianity has been the dominant religion of Europe and has spread through European colonialism to the Americas, Africa, and Asia. Today it claims more than two billion adherents and is the world's largest religion. It is also the most geographically diverse, with its center of gravity having shifted from Europe to Africa and Latin America.
beginner.theBoundaries:
Christianity's boundaries are defined by the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus — but what those events mean has been disputed since the beginning. The earliest Christians disagreed about Jesus's nature (was he human, divine, or both?), about how to relate to Jewish law, and about what salvation required. These debates produced councils, creeds, schisms, and eventually the major divisions of the tradition: Catholic, Orthodox, Protestant, and the hundreds of denominations within each.

The movement's expansion required continuous negotiation about what was essential and what could adapt. Christianity spread partly by absorbing local practices, and partly because European power imposed it on conquered peoples.
beginner.theConditions:
Christianity emerged from Judaism in a specific historical context: Roman-occupied Palestine, with its combination of Jewish religious ferment, Roman political power, and Hellenistic cultural influence. The Jewish tradition provided Christianity's scriptures, ethical framework, and concept of God. The Roman Empire provided the infrastructure — roads, shared language, urban networks — through which it spread. The Greek philosophical tradition provided the conceptual tools through which it articulated its theology.

The conditions that turned a small Jewish sect into a world religion are still debated. Key factors: the resurrection belief; the willingness to include Gentiles without circumcision; the organizational capacity of early communities; and Constantine's conversion in 312 CE.
beginner.internalDiversity:
Christianity has always been internally diverse. The New Testament itself contains multiple theological perspectives. The early church debated the nature of Christ for centuries before reaching a compromise formula (the Nicene Creed of 325 CE). The Great Schism of 1054 divided Eastern and Western Christianity. The Protestant Reformation of the sixteenth century shattered Western Christianity into thousands of denominations.

This diversity means 'Christianity' covers everything from Ethiopian Orthodox liturgy to American evangelical megachurches with rock bands, from liberation theology to prosperity gospel.
beginner.longConsequences:
Christianity's long consequences for world civilization are almost impossible to overstate. It shaped European law, education, art, philosophy, and political thought — through canon law, monastic schools, the synthesis of theology and Greek philosophy, and centuries of tension between church and state.

Through European colonialism, Christianity spread to every continent and became the dominant religion of the Americas and much of Africa and Asia. This spread involved both genuine conversion and violent imposition — the destruction of indigenous religious traditions alongside the construction of hospitals, schools, and social services. The legacy is inseparable from the history of colonial power.
beginner.periodizationDebate: Historians debate how to periodize Christianity's history and how to understand its relationship to other movements. Is Christianity continuous from the first century to the present? Or are there ruptures — the Constantinian establishment, the Great Schism, the Reformation — significant enough to constitute different religions under the same name? The diversity of contemporary Christianity makes this more than a historical question: what makes a self-described Christian movement authentically Christian is still contested.

general.thePeriod: Peter Brown's account of early Christianity — particularly in The Rise of Western Christendom — emphasizes how Christianity succeeded by being extraordinarily flexible in its early centuries: taking on local practices, incorporating philosophical traditions, and developing distinct communities that served specific social functions. This flexibility contrasts with later Christianity's claims to universality and uniformity. The early Christian movement was a plurality of movements that the institutional church gradually organized into a working unity — and never entirely succeeded.
general.theBoundaries: The Council of Nicaea (325 CE) — called by Constantine to resolve the Arian controversy about Christ's nature — established the precedent of imperial power defining Christian orthodoxy. The creed it produced ('begotten, not made, of one substance with the Father') was a philosophical formula that satisfied almost nobody entirely. The Arians who were excluded represented a majority in many regions at the time. The history of Christian orthodoxy is partly the history of conciliar decisions that produced minority traditions — Arians, Nestorians, Monophysites — that continued to exist outside official Christianity.
general.theConditions:
The Pauline contribution to early Christianity is as important as the Jesus tradition in shaping what the religion became. Paul's letters — the earliest Christian documents — developed the theology of Christ's death as atonement, the inclusion of Gentiles without Jewish law, and the organizational framework of house churches that spread across the empire. The Paul who shaped Christianity was a Hellenized Jew whose ability to move between Jewish and Greek worlds made him uniquely positioned to define a religion that would appeal to both.

Ramsay MacMullen's work on the Christianization of the Roman Empire complicates the standard narrative of gradual voluntary conversion. MacMullen shows that coercion — state enforcement of anti-pagan laws, mob violence against temples, episcopal pressure on local populations — played a significant role in Christianity's triumph. The Roman Empire did not simply choose Christianity through persuasion; it was also compelled toward it by a combination of imperial policy, Christian social pressure, and the systematic destruction of pagan alternatives.
general.internalDiversity:
The Reformation's long-term consequence was not just the division of Western Christianity but the eventual development of religious pluralism as a political value. Once Christians were killing each other over theological disputes, the argument for toleration — developed by Locke, Bayle, and others — gained force. The religious wars of the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries were the condition under which Enlightenment secularism became politically viable.

The global South's Christianity has developed theologies and practices that are increasingly distinct from European and North American versions. Pentecostalism, liberation theology, African independent churches, and charismatic Catholicism represent genuine theological innovations, not merely adaptations of European Christianity to local contexts. The center of gravity of global Christianity has shifted decisively southward. The number of Christians in Africa alone now exceeds the number in Europe, and the theologies emerging from the global South will shape the religion's future more than debates in Western churches.
general.longConsequences:
Christianity's shaping of European law extends from the medieval period through the present. Canon law — the law of the Church — was the most sophisticated legal system in medieval Europe and provided the conceptual framework for the development of secular common law and civil law. The concept of natural law — moral principles knowable by reason that apply to all humans — was developed by Aquinas within a Christian framework and became the basis for international law and human rights theory.

Through European colonialism, Christianity spread to every continent and became the dominant religion of the Americas and much of Africa and Asia. This spread involved both genuine conversion and violent imposition — the destruction of indigenous religious traditions alongside the construction of hospitals, schools, and social services. The legacy is inseparable from the history of colonial power. Contemporary debates about the return of cultural artifacts, the apology for past abuses, and the relationship between missionary Christianity and colonial administration all turn on how this history is evaluated.
general.periodizationDebate:
The question of Christianity's relationship to Judaism — its parent religion — is a contested historiographical question with contemporary political stakes. Early Christianity defined itself against Judaism (and Judaism defined itself, partly, against Christianity) in a process that scholars like Daniel Boyarin have called the 'partings of the ways.' The relationship was much messier and more gradual than the traditional narrative suggests, with Jewish-Christian communities persisting for centuries and clear boundaries established only late.

The relationship between Christianity and Judaism — its parent religion — is a contested historiographical question with ongoing political stakes. Early Christianity defined itself against Judaism, and Judaism defined itself partly against Christianity, in a process that scholars like Daniel Boyarin have called the 'partings of the ways.' The relationship was messier and more gradual than the traditional narrative suggests. Jewish-Christian communities persisted for centuries, and clear boundaries were established only late. The history of Christian anti-Semitism — which culminated in the Holocaust — makes this genealogical history politically charged in ways that purely historical treatments cannot fully contain.

advanced.thePeriod:
The historical Jesus — the Galilean preacher whose life and teachings generated the Christian movement — is known through documents written decades after his death by people who had not met him. The quest for the historical Jesus, which has occupied biblical scholars since the eighteenth century, has established some facts with reasonable confidence: he was a Jewish preacher in Roman Palestine, he attracted followers, he was crucified by Roman authorities, and his followers believed he had risen from the dead. Everything beyond this minimal core is contested.

The quest for the historical Jesus — which has occupied biblical scholars since the eighteenth century — has established some facts with reasonable confidence: he was a Jewish preacher in Roman Palestine, he attracted followers, he was crucified by Roman authorities, and his followers believed he had risen from the dead. Everything beyond this minimal core is contested. John Meier's four-volume A Marginal Jew applies the full toolkit of historical-critical method to establish what can be known. Bart Ehrman's more accessible accounts have brought the scholarly debates to a general audience while maintaining the essential distinction between historical evidence and theological belief.
advanced.theBoundaries:
Bart Ehrman's work on the variety of early Christianity — particularly Lost Christianities — has shown that the forms of Christianity that were declared heretical in the second and third centuries were not marginal deviations from an original orthodoxy but genuine competitors for the Christian tradition. Gnostic Christianity, Marcionite Christianity, Jewish Christianity — these were not perversions of an original Christianity but early developments that lost the competition to define the tradition. The winners wrote history; the losers' texts were suppressed or destroyed.

Bart Ehrman's work on early Christian diversity has shown that the forms of Christianity declared heretical in the second and third centuries were not marginal deviations from original orthodoxy but genuine competitors for the Christian tradition. Gnostic Christianity, Marcionite Christianity, Jewish Christianity — these were not perversions of an original form but early developments that lost the competition to define the tradition. The winners wrote history; the losers' texts were suppressed or destroyed. The discovery of the Nag Hammadi library in 1945 — a cache of Gnostic texts buried in the Egyptian desert — provided direct evidence of the traditions the proto-orthodox suppressed.

The relationship between the Council of Nicaea and Constantine's political interests has been debated since the council itself. Constantine did not make Christianity Rome's official religion — that happened under Theodosius in 380 CE — but he did use his authority to resolve theological disputes and to define orthodoxy. Whether the Nicene Creed represents theological truth or political compromise — or both — is a question that Christians have been arguing ever since. The historical account and the theological account of Nicaea's authority are genuinely different questions, though they are frequently conflated.
advanced.theConditions:
Ramsay MacMullen's work on the Christianization of the Roman Empire complicates the standard narrative of gradual voluntary conversion. MacMullen shows that coercion — state enforcement of anti-pagan laws, mob violence against temples, episcopal pressure on local populations — played a significant role in the triumph of Christianity. The Roman Empire did not simply choose Christianity; it was also compelled toward it by a combination of imperial policy, Christian social pressure, and the destruction of alternatives.

The sociology of early Christianity's growth has been analyzed systematically by Rodney Stark, whose The Rise of Christianity (1997) applies sociological models to explain why Christianity grew faster than competing religious movements. Stark's key factors: Christianity's high retention of women (who had higher status in Christian communities than in pagan ones), its effective care for the sick during epidemics (which increased survival rates and gratitude), and its clear boundaries of belonging (which created strong in-group solidarity). Whether these factors sufficiently explain Christianity's growth without reference to its theological content is contested, but Stark's framework shifted the terms of the historical debate.
advanced.internalDiversity:
The global South's Christianity — particularly in Africa and Latin America — has developed theologies and practices that are increasingly distinct from European and North American Christianity. Pentecostalism, liberation theology, African independent churches, and charismatic Catholicism represent genuine theological innovations, not simply versions of European Christianity adapted to local contexts. The center of gravity of global Christianity has shifted decisively from Europe and North America to the global South, and the theologies emerging there will shape the religion's future more than the debates of Western churches.

The feminist critique of Christian institutions has engaged seriously with both the egalitarian potential of Christian theology (Paul's claim that 'there is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female') and the patriarchal institutions that have characterized most Christian traditions. Feminist biblical scholarship — figures like Elisabeth Schüssler Fiorenza and Phyllis Trible — has recovered the roles of women in early Christianity that were suppressed in canonical texts and by institutional church development. The ordination of women, which divides Roman Catholicism and many evangelical traditions from mainline Protestant denominations, represents the live contemporary version of this historical debate.
advanced.longConsequences:
Diarmaid MacCulloch's A History of Christianity (2009) is the most comprehensive recent synthesis, covering the full two-thousand-year history with attention to non-Western traditions usually marginalized in standard accounts. MacCulloch emphasizes the diversity and contingency of Christian history — the many points at which things could have gone differently — and resists the teleological narrative in which the tradition moves toward its present form. His account of the Eastern churches, of African Christianity, and of Christianity's global spread gives the religion's history a genuinely global rather than Eurocentric frame.

Christianity's shaping of European law extends from the medieval period through the present. Canon law — the law of the Church — was the most sophisticated legal system in medieval Europe and provided the conceptual framework for the development of secular common law and civil law. The concept of natural law — moral principles knowable by reason that apply to all humans — was developed by Aquinas within a Christian framework and became the basis for international law and human rights theory. The relationship between Christian natural law and secular human rights doctrine is contested: whether the latter derives from the former or represents a genuinely secular alternative is both a philosophical and a political question.
advanced.periodizationDebate:
Diarmaid MacCulloch's A History of Christianity (2009) is the most comprehensive recent synthesis, covering the full two-thousand-year history with attention to non-Western traditions that are usually marginalized. MacCulloch's account emphasizes the diversity and contingency of Christian history — the many points at which things could have gone differently — and resists the teleological narrative in which the tradition moves toward its present form.

The relationship between Christianity and the development of science has been contested since the Galileo affair. The older 'conflict' narrative — Christianity systematically suppressing science — has been largely rejected by historians of science. The newer 'complexity' narrative emphasizes that Christianity both provided the institutional framework for early modern science (universities, learned societies) and created the theological problems (the nature of Providence, the reading of scripture) that generated conflicts. The Galileo case was as much a conflict about authority and institutional power as about the content of scientific claims.

The global South's emergence as Christianity's center of gravity has forced a rethinking of how the religion's history is periodized. For two centuries, Christianity's history was told as a story of European expansion: from the Mediterranean origins, through the Roman Empire, through medieval European consolidation, through Reformation and Enlightenment, to modernity. This periodization makes the current globalization of Christianity look like a recent development. But African Christianity (Egypt and Ethiopia) and Asian Christianity (Syria, India) have ancient roots that predate European Christianity's dominance. A genuinely global periodization would look quite different from the standard European one.

foundation.theStory: Christianity began as a Jewish sect and became a world religion through a series of transformations that were neither inevitable nor planned: the inclusion of Gentiles, the conversion of Constantine, the development of a theological tradition that could speak to Greek philosophy, and eventually the spread through European colonialism to every continent. Understanding these transformations is essential for understanding Western civilization and the contemporary world.
foundation.theDebate: The central scholarly debates concern the historical Jesus (what can be known about him?), the origins of Christianity (why did this particular Jewish sect survive and expand while others did not?), the nature of early Christian diversity (was there an original orthodoxy that heresies deviated from, or multiple competing versions?), and the role of coercion versus conversion in Christianity's expansion.
foundation.whyItMatters: Christianity matters as the religion that shaped Western civilization's art, law, philosophy, and political thought, and as the religion whose spread through colonialism transformed the Americas, Africa, and Asia. Understanding Christianity is essential for understanding European history, the history of colonialism, contemporary global politics, and the ongoing debates about religion's role in public life.
questions: Christianity spread first through the Roman Empire's urban networks — along trade routes, through port cities, in the households of merchants and soldiers. What does this tell us about how new religious movements spread? Is the social infrastructure of transmission more important than the content of the message?

The Constantinian establishment — Christianity becoming Rome's official religion — changed the movement in fundamental ways. What was gained and what was lost when a persecuted sect became a state religion? Is there a sense in which Christianity was corrupted by political success?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The key scholarly works span biblical studies (Ehrman, Meier, Crossan on the historical Jesus), patristics (Brown on late antiquity and early Christianity), church history (MacCulloch's comprehensive synthesis), and sociology of religion (Rodney Stark on Christianity's growth). The debate between 'conflict' and 'complexity' accounts of Christianity and science (Lindberg and Numbers) is important for understanding the Galileo affair and its legacy. Feminist and post-colonial scholarship has substantially revised the standard narrative by recovering marginalized voices.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for early Christianity is primarily textual — the New Testament, the apostolic fathers, the church fathers. Archaeological evidence supplements but rarely overturns textual evidence. The limits: the earliest Christian texts were written decades after the events they describe, by people with theological commitments that shaped what they recorded. The quest for the historical Jesus is a quest to recover a figure through sources that are theological documents, not historical reports.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Christianity must specify which period, tradition, and question it is addressing. Claims about 'Christianity' as a unified phenomenon across two thousand years and hundreds of traditions are analytically weak. The most productive scholarship focuses on specific transformations: the Pauline reinterpretation of the Jesus movement, the Constantinian establishment, the Gregorian Reform, the Reformation, the global South's emergence as Christianity's center of gravity.
questions: Christianity developed a sophisticated philosophical theology through engagement with Greek philosophy — particularly Platonism and Aristotelianism. Did this theological development clarify Christian belief or distort it? Is the God of the Nicene Creed the same God as the God of the New Testament?

Liberation theology — developed in Latin America in the 1960s-70s — argued that God has a preferential option for the poor and that Christian faith requires political commitment to social justice. The Vatican under John Paul II suppressed liberation theology. What does this conflict reveal about the relationship between Christian theology and political power?

research.topic: The historical Jesus — minimal established facts
research.content: Historical Jesus scholarship has established a minimal core of facts about Jesus of Nazareth with reasonable confidence: he was a Jewish preacher in Roman Palestine, probably active in the late 20s CE; he attracted followers; he was baptized by John the Baptist; he gathered disciples; he was crucified by Roman authorities under Pontius Pilate; his followers believed he had appeared to them after his death. These facts are accepted by the overwhelming majority of scholars, including non-Christian scholars. Everything beyond this core — the content of his teaching, his self-understanding, his miracles — is contested.

Scholarship since the discovery of the Nag Hammadi library (1945) has established that early Christianity was significantly more diverse than the standard narrative suggests. The texts from Nag Hammadi show that Gnostic Christianity was a sophisticated theological tradition with its own scriptures and communities, not a marginal deviation from orthodoxy. Bart Ehrman's synthesis of this scholarship has made it widely accessible. The scholarly consensus now treats 'orthodoxy' as a retrospective category — the winner's version of what Christianity should have been — rather than a description of an original unity from which heresies deviated.

research.topic: The experiences and beliefs of ordinary Christians in the early period
research.content: What ordinary Christians believed, practiced, and experienced in the first three centuries CE cannot be recovered with precision. The surviving texts are almost entirely from educated elite Christians — bishops, theologians, apologists — whose version of Christianity may have differed significantly from the beliefs and practices of ordinary communities. What evidence exists: archaeological material (burial practices, religious objects, graffiti), papyrus documents from Egypt, and references in elite texts to popular beliefs that they found objectionable. What cannot be recovered: the inner religious experience, the specific practices of household churches, and the beliefs of Christians who left no written record.

comparativeNarrative.name: Jewish Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: From within the Jewish tradition, Christianity represents a movement that emerged from Judaism, claimed the Jewish scriptures, and then turned against Jews — supersessionism (the claim that the Church replaced Israel in God's covenant) becoming the theological basis for centuries of persecution. The Jewish-Christian relationship is not simply one of two separate religions developing in parallel but of a daughter religion that defined itself partly through the rejection and subordination of its parent. The Holocaust — enabled by centuries of Christian anti-Semitism — is the catastrophic endpoint of this relationship.

comparativeNarrative.name: African Christianity Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: African Christianity — both the ancient traditions of Egypt and Ethiopia and the rapidly growing contemporary churches of sub-Saharan Africa — represents a version of the religion that is often invisible in standard narratives dominated by European Christianity. Africa was central to early Christianity: Alexandria was one of its intellectual centers, Augustine was North African, the Desert Fathers of Egypt developed monastic tradition. The Christianity of contemporary Africa — often Pentecostal, charismatic, and deeply embedded in local cultures — is the fastest-growing form of the religion and will shape its future.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Indigenous peoples subjected to Christian missions
comparativeNarrative.name: Indigenous Perspective
comparativeNarrative.content: For indigenous peoples of the Americas, Africa, and Asia, Christian missions arrived with or immediately after colonial conquest. The relationship between gospel and gun — the cross and the sword — was not accidental but structural: missions served colonial power by civilizing the natives (destroying indigenous cultures and languages), justifying conquest (the natives needed to be saved), and providing an administrative network (mission stations as colonial outposts). Contemporary indigenous Christians grapple with how to hold a faith that was partly imposed through violence.

rabbitHole.label: The Roman Empire
rabbitHole.reason: Christianity spread through the Roman Empire's infrastructure and was transformed by its eventual adoption as Rome's official religion — the empire and the religion shaped each other fundamentally.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: Islam and Christianity share Abrahamic roots, contested claims about Jesus, and a history of conflict and mutual definition — understanding one illuminates the other.

rabbitHole.label: The Protestant Reformation
rabbitHole.reason: The Reformation shattered Western Christianity and produced the religious pluralism that eventually required the development of toleration and secularism as political principles.

rabbitHole.label: Augustine of Hippo
rabbitHole.reason: Augustine's theology — original sin, grace, the relationship between the earthly and heavenly cities — shaped Western Christianity more than any other thinker after Paul.

rabbitHole.label: The Crusades
rabbitHole.reason: The Crusades represent the most violent expression of medieval Christianity's political ambitions and shaped Christian-Muslim relations for centuries.

reference.author: MacCulloch, Diarmaid
reference.title: A History of Christianity: The First Three Thousand Years
reference.annotation: The essential comprehensive history — MacCulloch's 1,100-page synthesis covers Christianity from its origins to the present with remarkable balance and attention to non-Western traditions.

reference.author: Brown, Peter
reference.title: The Rise of Western Christendom
reference.annotation: The essential account of early medieval Christianity — Brown's synthesis shows how Christianity spread through and transformed late antique civilization, with particular attention to the varieties of local Christian practice.

reference.author: Ehrman, Bart
reference.title: Lost Christianities: The Battles for Scripture and the Faiths We Never Knew
reference.annotation: The essential account of early Christian diversity — Ehrman's accessible synthesis of the Nag Hammadi discoveries and the variety of early Christianity provides the essential corrective to the standard narrative of original orthodoxy.

reference.author: Meier, John P.
reference.title: A Marginal Jew: Rethinking the Historical Jesus (4 vols.)
reference.annotation: The most rigorous historical Jesus scholarship — Meier's four-volume work applies the full toolkit of historical-critical method to the New Testament sources and produces the most careful account of what can be known about Jesus.

reference.author: Lane Fox, Robin
reference.title: Pagans and Christians
reference.annotation: The essential account of Christianity's competition with paganism — Lane Fox's history of the first three centuries shows how Christianity triumphed in a religious marketplace and what the triumph cost.

reference.author: MacMullen, Ramsay
reference.title: Christianizing the Roman Empire (A.D. 100–400)
reference.annotation: The essential account of how Christianization actually worked — MacMullen's emphasis on coercion alongside conversion complicates the standard narrative of peaceful voluntary conversion.

commerce.title: A History of Christianity
commerce.author: Diarmaid MacCulloch
commerce.note: The best single starting point — comprehensive, readable, and intellectually honest about the tradition's complexity and failures.

commerce.title: How Jesus Became God
commerce.author: Bart Ehrman
commerce.note: The best accessible account of early Christian theology — Ehrman traces how the understanding of Jesus developed from human prophet to divine figure in the first centuries of the movement.

commerce.title: The Rise of Western Christendom
commerce.author: Peter Brown
commerce.note: Essential for late antiquity and early medieval Christianity — Brown's account shows how Christianity absorbed and transformed Roman civilization.

commerce.title: Lost Christianities
commerce.author: Bart Ehrman
commerce.note: Essential for early diversity — Ehrman's account of the competing Christianities that lost the battle for orthodoxy is the best introduction to the complexity of early Christian history.

commerce.title: A Marginal Jew
commerce.author: John Meier
commerce.note: The most rigorous historical Jesus scholarship — essential for anyone who wants to understand what the historical-critical method can and cannot establish about Jesus.

commerce.title: Pagans and Christians
commerce.author: Robin Lane Fox
commerce.note: The essential account of Christianity's competition with paganism — exhaustive, scholarly, and the best treatment of how Christianity triumphed in the religious marketplace of late antiquity.

popularCulture.title: The Last Temptation of Christ (1988) / The Passion of the Christ (2004)
popularCulture.description: These two films represent opposite ends of the spectrum of Jesus representations — Scorsese's psychologically complex, humanized Jesus drew protests from conservative Christians; Gibson's graphic crucifixion film drew protests from those concerned about anti-Semitic tropes. Both films generated controversy precisely because Christianity's core events are not merely historical but charged with ongoing theological and political significance.

popularCulture.title: Christian holidays in secular culture
popularCulture.description: Christmas and Easter have become secular cultural events in Western countries whose populations are no longer majority Christian — the Christmas shopping season, Easter eggs, and associated rituals persist as cultural practices after their religious significance has faded for many participants. This persistence illustrates how deeply Christianity shaped Western culture: even secularization leaves Christian temporal rhythms, architectural forms, and moral vocabulary in place.

popularCulture.title: Liberation theology and political Christianity
popularCulture.description: Liberation theology — developed in Latin America in the 1960s and 70s by theologians like Gustavo Gutiérrez — argued that God has a preferential option for the poor and that authentic Christian faith requires political commitment to justice. It has influenced the Black church tradition in the United States, Catholic social teaching, and contemporary progressive Christianity. Pope Francis's emphasis on poverty and inequality reflects the liberation theology tradition that his predecessors suppressed.

popularCulture.title: The Young Pope / The New Pope (HBO, 2016-2019)
popularCulture.description: Paolo Sorrentino's television series about a fictional American pope engages seriously with the theological and political tensions within contemporary Catholicism — between tradition and reform, between authority and mercy, between European Christianity's decline and the global South's rise. Unusual in treating Christianity's internal debates with intellectual seriousness rather than as background to a political thriller.

popularCulture.title: The prosperity gospel as American Christianity
popularCulture.description: The prosperity gospel — teaching that faith and tithing produce wealth and health — treats material success as divine favor, reversing traditional Christian suspicion of wealth. It has become one of the fastest-growing forms of Christianity globally, spreading from the United States to Africa, Latin America, and Asia. Whether it exploits the poor or offers genuine hope is debated; what is clear is that it has reshaped how millions understand Christianity's relationship to money.
